name: Analysis — where my money went & how the trend is moving
summary: Opening Analysis and reading a truthful picture of spend + net-worth trend. The rendering rungs work; the BROKEN rungs are the filters underneath — three tiles answer 'how much did I spend' with three different WHERE clauses, and the trend tiles compare an all-time total (or a stale snapshot) against a monthly baseline.

steps.title: Tap Analysis tab
steps.does: You open the Analysis surface from the bottom nav.
steps.plain: The entry point. In Postman terms this is selecting the Analysis collection — the screen you'll fire all the read-only 'report' requests from. goPage just swaps which screen is visible, then calls renderAll().

steps.title: renderAll fans out to the Analysis renderers
steps.does: Because pg-spend is now the active screen, the master render pass calls the three Analysis builders.
steps.wired: renderAll() → if pg-spend active { renderAnalysisTab(); renderSurvivalForecast(); renderSpendingPivot(); }
steps.plain: One dispatcher fires several report queries at once. Each builder is its own endpoint reading the same txn table — and that's where the trouble starts: they don't all use the same WHERE clause.

steps.title: Spending pivot renders — 'WHERE YOUR MONEY WENT'
steps.does: The top tile draws category cards with a total-outflows header and a Today / 7 Days / 30 Days / All time period toggle.
steps.plain: The headline report. The card layout, bars and toggle are healthy — this rung works. The number it shows is sourced by the filter in the next step.

steps.title: Pivot total uses the LAX outflow filter
steps.does: buildSpendingPivot groups txns via getAllOutflowsByCategory — only drops income, corrections and round-ups. Bills / Loan / CC Payment / Savings / Transfer ALL count as 'spend'.
steps.plain: Here's the first cracked rung. This query forgot to exclude the non-discretionary rows — its WHERE clause only filters out income/corrections. So on a bill-pay day this tile's 'today' total balloons. Meanwhile the Dashboard 'spent today' uses the STRICT filter (computeSpentInRange / _NON_SPEND_CATS, 8 excluded cats). Same question, two endpoints, two answers — OPEN-BUGS #6/#8 filter scatter.

steps.title: Toggle the period (Today / 7d / 30d / All)
steps.does: Tapping a period chip re-queries the pivot for that window.
steps.plain: The time-window selector works mechanically — it just narrows the WHERE clause's date range. But because step 4's filter is lax, every window inherits the same over-count.

steps.title: Category breakdown tile renders
steps.does: renderCatBreakdown draws a second 'where it went' list labelled 'This month (all logged)' — but it queries ALL-TIME, not this month, and uses the same lax outflow filter.
steps.plain: A broken rung in two ways. The label says 'This month' but the date range is from 0 (the dawn of time) to now — so a returning user sees lifetime totals under a monthly heading. And it's the same lax filter as the pivot, so it can disagree with both the Dashboard AND the strict Cut-sliders tile sitting just below it. This is the 'today's spend shows 3 different numbers' class.

steps.title: Cut sliders tile — STRICT discretionary filter
steps.does: 'Where to cut' reads STRICT discretionary by category (excludes Bills/Loan/CC/Transfer + corrections/roundups) and compares each category to a monthly baseline.
steps.plain: This rung is healthy AND correctly opinionated: for 'what should I cut' it deliberately uses the strict WHERE clause (don't suggest cutting a loan repayment). The bug isn't here — it's that the tile ABOVE it (step 6) uses a different filter, so the two tiles on the same screen disagree on totals. OPEN-BUGS #7 (all-time-vs-monthly) was patched here by moving to cycle-scoped MODEL data; baselines remain hardcoded monthly.

steps.title: Change the segment (1m / 3m / 6m)
steps.does: Tapping a segment chip multiplies the projected savings over 1, 3 or 6 months and re-renders the cut sliders.
steps.plain: A projection multiplier — the only step on this surface that writes anything (it stores which horizon you picked). It scales the strict per-category overage by 1/3/6 months. Works correctly.

steps.title: renderTrend — month-on-month category deltas
steps.does: renderTrend compares each category's spend to last month's snapshot — but 'this' side is ALL-TIME outflows, while the comparison baseline is a single monthlyHistory snapshot.
steps.plain: Broken rung: it diffs an all-time running total against a point-in-time monthly snapshot. As lifetime spend only ever grows, the delta vs 'last month' inflates over time — the same shape as comparing a cumulative ledger balance to a single period's figure. Renders fine, reads wrong.

steps.title: Month-on-Month tile (inside Analysis tab)
steps.does: renderAnalysisTab draws a per-category '% vs last month' list using STRICT discretionary spend vs the last monthlyHistory snapshot.
steps.plain: This tile is healthy — it filters strict (excludes _NON_SPEND_CATS) and only shows a delta when last month's value exists. Note it can still disagree with step 9's renderTrend, which uses the lax all-time filter — two 'month-on-month' tiles, two methods.

steps.title: Net Worth Trend — 'vs last month'
steps.does: Reads the latest monthlyHistory snapshot's liquidNet and subtracts it from live net worth to show the 'vs last month' delta.
steps.wired: renderAnalysisTab() NW block → latest = monthlyHistory[last]; delta = nw.liquidNet - latest.liquidNet
steps.file: index.html:6402, baseline read index.html:6414, delta index.html:6422
steps.plain: The famous '+$90k vs last month' rung. The baseline is whatever sits in the last monthlyHistory row — and if that snapshot is stale or pre-dates a big asset entry (WRX, Super), the delta is nonsense. A $15k SANITY_CAP guard now hides implausible deltas behind a 'trend stabilising' message instead of printing the wrong number, but the underlying read is still against a possibly-wrong baseline. Like reporting month-over-month change against a snapshot that was never refreshed.

steps.title: Net Worth Trend delta passes the sanity gate
steps.does: When the snapshot is fresh and the delta is within plausible bounds, the green/red '+/- $X vs last month' line renders correctly.
steps.wired: renderAnalysisTab() NW block → isPlausible guard then delta line
steps.plain: The happy path. With a current snapshot the math is right and the guard lets the real number through. This is the rung working as intended — the breakage in step 11 is entirely about whether the baseline snapshot is trustworthy.
